There is no more sad or surprising example of human wickedness in the sacred Scriptures than what is recorded here. Solomon became a public worshipper of disgusting idols! He likely gradually gave way to pride and luxury, and thus lost his appreciation for true wisdom. Nothing protects us by itself from the deceitfulness and wickedness of the human heart. Nor will old age cure the heart of any bad habit. If our sinful passions are not put to death and controlled by God’s grace, they never will die on their own, but will last even when opportunities to indulge them are removed. Let anyone who thinks he is secure take care that he does not fall. We see how weak we are without the grace of God; let us therefore live in constant dependence on that grace. Let us watch and be sensible: ours is a dangerous battle, and we are in an enemy’s land, while our worst enemies are the betrayers within our own hearts.

The Lord told Solomon, likely by a prophet, what he must expect for his turning away from God. Although we have reason to hope that he repented and found mercy, the Holy Spirit did not expressly record it, but left it uncertain as a warning to others not to sin. The guilt may be removed, but not the shame; that will remain. Thus it must remain uncertain to us until the day of judgment whether or not Solomon was left to suffer the everlasting displeasure of an offended God.

While Solomon stayed close to God and to his duty, there was no enemy to cause him trouble; but here we have an account of two. If they are against us, he can make even the least frightening, and a small insect will be burdensome. Although they were motivated by ambition or revenge, God used them to correct Solomon.

In telling the reason why God divided the kingdom from Solomon’s house, Ahijah warned Jeroboam to take care not to sin away his position. Yet the house of David must be supported; out of it the Messiah would arise. Solomon tried to kill his successor. Had he not taught others that whatever plans are in people's hearts, the counsel of the Lord will stand? Yet he himself thinks to defeat that counsel. Jeroboam withdrew into Egypt and was content to live in exile and obscurity for a while, being sure of a kingdom eventually. Should we not be content, who have a better kingdom in reserve?

Solomon's reign was as long as his father’s, but his life was not. Sin shortened his days. If the world, with all its advantages, could satisfy the soul and afford real joy, Solomon would have found it so. But he was disappointed in everything, and to warn us, has left this record of all earthly pleasures: “Vanity and vexation of spirit.” The New Testament declares that someone greater than Solomon has come to reign over us and to possess the throne of his father David. May we not see something of Christ's excellence faintly represented to us in this image?
